g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 4: Introduction to Network Layer. This layer is Layer 3 of the OSI model and enables communication across different networks.
The Network Layer sits at Layer 3 in the OSI model, between the Data Link Layer and Transport Layer, providing logical addressing and routing.
The Network Layer is crucial because it enables communication between devices on different networks, forming the backbone of the internet.
The Network Layer performs four main functions: Logical Addressing, Routing, Forwarding, and Fragmentation for end-to-end packet delivery.
Key protocols at the Network Layer include IP for addressing, ARP for IP-to-MAC resolution, and ICMP for error reporting.
IPv4 addresses are 32-bit numbers with two parts: network portion identifying the network, and host portion identifying the specific device.
A subnet mask is a 32-bit number that determines which portion of an IP address is the network and which is the host using bitwise AND operation.
Classful IPv4 addressing divides the address space into five classes: A, B, C for unicast, D for multicast, and E for experimental.
To find network address: perform IP AND Subnet Mask. For broadcast address: perform Network OR (NOT Subnet Mask).
For subnetting with 200+ hosts per subnet, we need 8 host bits, giving /24 mask and 256 subnets from a /16 network.
To check if hosts are on the same subnet, apply the subnet mask to both IPs and compare the resulting network addresses.
Routers use routing tables to determine the best path for packets. Routing protocols like OSPF, BGP, and RIP populate these tables dynamically.
IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses versus IPv4's 32 bits, providing virtually unlimited addressing with built-in security and auto-configuration.
Key exam tips: memorize OSI layers, practice subnetting, understand routing vs forwarding, know ARP and TTL functions.
In summary, the Network Layer provides logical addressing, routing, and forwarding to enable communication across different networks using IP protocols.
g.text: • Network Layer = Layer 3 | Functions: Logical Addressing, Routing, Forwarding, Fragmentation
• Network = IP AND Mask | Broadcast = Network OR ~Mask | ARP resolves IP→MAC | TTL prevents loops
